_At 96, Asia’s most famous self-made billionaire finds himself at the center of a geopolitical storm, caught between Washington, Beijing, and the legacy of an empire built from nothing._

By Editorial Staff \| Global Times

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*12Y62cEeIymPsfVDaIoJTA@2x.jpeg)

He built his fortune from plastic flowers and turned it into one of the world’s most sprawling conglomerates. He survived wars, refugee camps, colonial rule, and the convulsions of modern China’s rise. He has been called a genius, a traitor, a patriot, and a coward, sometimes in the same week. At 96, Li Ka-shing, the man his admirers nicknamed ‘Superman,’ remains impossible to pin down.

Today, that elusiveness has placed him at the precise epicenter of the U.S.-China trade war, after his flagship company, CK Hutchison Holdings, announced a deal to sell 43 ports in 23 countries including two strategic positions at either end of the Panama Canal — to a BlackRock-led consortium for $22.8 billion. The move has infuriated Beijing, earned cautious praise from Washington, and reignited every debate ever had about what Li Ka-shing truly is: a Chinese patriot, a Western-facing capitalist, or simply a man who has always played every side with surgical precision.

_“Don’t be naive and senile.”_ — Chinese State Council’s Hong Kong and Macao Affairs Office, addressing Li Ka-shing, March 2025

## From Refugee to ‘Superman’: A Life Against All Odds

Li Ka-shing was born on July 29, 1928, in Chao’an, a coastal city in Guangdong Province, China. His father, Li Yun-ching, was a respected schoolteacher, and young Ka-shing showed an early aptitude for learning. The family’s comfortable life unraveled rapidly when Japanese forces invaded China in 1937.

In 1940, when Li was twelve years old, his family fled to Hong Kong as refugees, joining a wave of displaced mainland Chinese seeking safety under British colonial rule. Within three years of arriving, his father contracted tuberculosis and died, leaving the teenage Li as the primary breadwinner for his mother and siblings. He dropped out of school permanently.

Li found work at a plastics trading company, famously working shifts of 16 hours or more a day to support his family. His diligence caught the attention of his employers, and by the age of 18 he had been promoted to factory manager, an almost unheard-of advancement for someone so young. By 1950, at just 22, he had saved enough to start his own business: Cheung Kong Industries, initially manufacturing plastic flowers for export.

In one of his most celebrated business maneuvers, Li spotted an Italian magazine article describing a new trend in plastic floral manufacturing and immediately began producing them before any competitor had caught on. The gambit made him a wealthy man. But plastic flowers were only the beginning.

## Building an Empire: From Real Estate to Global Ports

In the 1960s and 70s, Li pivoted aggressively into Hong Kong real estate, correctly identifying that the colonial government’s land policies would create a chronic shortage of property and that prices could only go in one direction. Through his newly formed Cheung Kong Holdings, he snapped up land and development rights with remarkable foresight.

The boldest move came in 1979, when Li acquired Hutchison Whampoa from the Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation, making him the first Chinese businessman to take over one of Hong Kong’s historic British trading houses, known as hongs. It was a watershed moment, both commercially and symbolically. The acquisition gave Li control over a vast portfolio: container ports, telecommunications, retail chains, and energy assets that spanned multiple continents.

His business empire eventually touched almost every aspect of life in Hong Kong: supermarkets, power utilities, mobile networks, pharmacies, and skyscrapers. Globally, the Hutchison brand spread to Europe, where it owns the drugstore chain Superdrug in the United Kingdom and operates the Three mobile phone network across several European nations. Hasbro, the American toy giant, was once among his factory’s clients.

Li’s rise mirrored Hong Kong’s own transformation from refugee waystation to global financial hub. By the early 2000s, AsiaWeek had declared him ‘the most powerful man in Asia.’ Forbes magazine eventually ranked him among the world’s 50 wealthiest individuals, and as of the March 2024 Forbes list, his net worth stood at approximately $37.3 billion.

## The Children: Victor, Richard, and the Question of Succession

Li Ka-shing married Chong Yuet-ming in 1962. She was, unusually for the time, his first cousin the daughter of his maternal uncle. Their union was considered deeply traditional, rooted in the customs of their Chaoshan heritage. Together they had two sons, Victor and Richard, both of whom became prominent figures in the Hong Kong business world. Both hold Canadian citizenship, educated partly abroad, a detail that would later carry symbolic weight.

Chong Yuet-ming died suddenly on New Year’s Day, 1990 officially from cardiac arrest, though some accounts have cited other causes. Li never remarried, and those who know him say the loss was profound and lasting. He has spoken sparingly about his personal grief over the decades.

Victor Li Tzar-kuoi, born in 1964, is the elder and more conservative of the two sons. He studied civil engineering at Stanford University and returned to work directly alongside his father at Cheung Kong and Hutchison Whampoa. Victor was always understood to be the chosen heir to the family’s core business empire, a widely anticipated succession that his father formalized in 2018 when he retired at 89, handing Victor the chairmanship of CK Hutchison Holdings. Victor also chairs CK Asset Holdings and CK Infrastructure Holdings.

Victor’s life has not been without drama. In 1996, he was kidnapped by the notorious Hong Kong gangster Cheung Tze-keung, better known as ‘Big Spender,’ on his way home from work. Li Ka-shing reportedly paid a ransom of HK$1.038 billion directly to Cheung, who had the audacity to come to the Li family home to collect it. Strikingly, no police report was ever filed in Hong Kong. Instead, mainland authorities, operating under a legal system that allowed for the death penalty pursued the case, and Cheung was executed in 1998. The episode has long fueled speculation about the depth of Li Ka-shing’s ties to mainland power structures, though he has consistently deflected such insinuations.

Richard Li Tzar-kai, born in 1966, is the younger and by most accounts more entrepreneurial and volatile of the two brothers. He attended Menlo School in California and began computer engineering at Stanford before withdrawing after three years. He is a licensed pilot in both the United States and Canada, and a certified divemaster. In the 1990s, Richard founded STAR TV, a pan-Asian satellite television network that he eventually sold to Rupert Murdoch’s News Corporation for $950 million — a deal that confirmed his independent bona fides. He later established Pacific Century Group, which controls PCCW, Hong Kong’s largest telecommunications company, and founded the FWD Group insurance empire. Forbes estimated his net worth at $4.5 billion in 2020.

Richard’s personal life attracted significant tabloid attention when his then-girlfriend Isabella Leong, a Macanese singer and actress who was 18 years his junior, gave birth to their son Ethan in 2008, followed by twin sons in 2010. The couple ended their relationship in 2011, with both committing to shared parenting. The relationship had drawn intense media scrutiny given the age gap and Leong’s celebrity status in the Chinese-speaking world.

The two brothers have generally operated separate business empires rather than a unified family front. Their public collaboration has been rare, with one notable exception: the 2019 restoration of the 1881 Heritage complex in Hong Kong, a former Marine Police Headquarters that Victor and Richard co-developed into a boutique hotel and restaurant destination. Victor has remained loyal to the family’s core conglomerate structure, while Richard has pursued higher-risk venture investments, a divergence that some observers see as a deliberate hedge by their father.

## Controversies: The Man Who Always Seemed to Know When to Leave

Li Ka-shing’s critics have long accused him of exceptional sensitivity to political winds, a talent that has enabled him to preserve and grow his wealth but that has sometimes looked, in the eyes of others, like opportunism or even disloyalty.

The most significant controversy erupted in 2015 when Li began quietly selling off mainland Chinese assets and restructuring his corporate entities, re-registering key holdings in the Cayman Islands. The maneuver attracted immediate fire from a think tank affiliated with Xinhua, China’s official state news agency, which published a widely circulated article with the accusatory title ‘Don’t let Li Ka-shing run away!’ The piece argued that Li owed his early fortune partly to Beijing’s patronage during the reform era under Deng Xiaoping and Jiang Zemin, and that moving money out of China during a period of economic anxiety was morally indefensible. Li’s response was characteristic: restrained, brief, and unapologetic.

Then came the 2019 pro-democracy protests, the most turbulent period in Hong Kong’s post-handover history. Li found himself under attack from both sides. Pro-Beijing voices criticized him for his perceived ambivalence; he published a public statement that called for the government to respond to protesters’ concerns, but stopped far short of endorsing their demands or condemning the police response. The statement was carefully parsed for any signal of where Li’s sympathies truly lay. Some read it as a quiet expression of support for democratic values; others called it fence-sitting of the most convenient kind. Pro-democracy activists hoped for a stronger endorsement; pro-Beijing commentators felt betrayed. Li had managed, somehow, to disappoint nearly everyone.

His relationship with successive Chinese leadership has followed a clear arc. During the Deng Xiaoping and Jiang Zemin eras, Li was considered an essential bridge between Beijing and international capital markets , a Chinese success story that the Communist Party could hold up as evidence that patriotism and prosperity were compatible. He met regularly with top Chinese leaders and served on the elite committee that selected Hong Kong’s Chief Executive. That influence began to visibly wane after Xi Jinping consolidated power in 2012. Li was removed from the Election Committee that chooses Hong Kong’s leader in 2021, in changes Beijing described as necessary to ensure ‘patriots’ governed Hong Kong.

## The Panama Ports Deal: A Final Act of Defiance?

On March 4, 2025, CK Hutchison announced what would instantly become the most controversial business transaction Li’s family had ever attempted: the sale of its entire 80% stake in Hutchison Ports, comprising 43 ports in 23 countries to a consortium led by BlackRock’s Global Infrastructure Partners, alongside Terminal Investment Limited, an Italian shipping group. The deal valued the port empire at approximately $22.8 billion.

The timing was electric. U.S. President Donald Trump had spent weeks insisting that the Panama Canal was effectively under Chinese control, and demanding it be ‘returned’ to American influence. Within days of the announcement, CK Hutchison’s shares soared. Then Beijing reacted.

The Chinese State Council’s Hong Kong and Macao Affairs Office circulated a scathing commentary calling on Li, described with barely veiled contempt as someone who had grown ‘naive and senile’ to reconsider. Ta Kung Pao, a pro-Beijing newspaper in Hong Kong, published more than ten commentaries in rapid succession, calling the deal an act of ‘spineless grovelling’ and accusing CK Hutchison of ‘betraying all Chinese people.’ Senior Chinese leaders ordered multiple government agencies, including the State Administration for Market Regulation, to open a formal review of the transaction for potential national security and antitrust violations, an extraordinary intervention in what Li’s camp had characterized as a purely commercial decision.

Victor Li was summoned to meet with a senior Chinese leader during the annual ‘two sessions’ parliamentary period, an unusual audience given Victor’s relatively modest political rank as a CPPCC member. He reportedly told the Chinese official that the ports were effectively being sold to an Italian firm, not BlackRock, pointing to TiL Group’s parent company Mediterranean Shipping Company and its founder Gianluigi Aponte, a longtime personal friend of his father.

The framing was a diplomatic attempt to soften the optics: this was not Li Ka-shing choosing America over China, but a global asset reshuffling with Italian shipping interests at its heart. Beijing remained unpersuaded. By April 2025, CK Hutchison found itself in the remarkable position of being simultaneously threatened by Washington for its historical ties to China and condemned by Beijing for selling to American interests.

The episode crystallized what many analysts had suspected for years: that Li Ka-shing had been methodically reducing his exposure to China-linked risk for over a decade, and that the Panama ports deal was less a geopolitical statement than the logical culmination of a long, private strategic retreat. CK Hutchison still earned roughly 12% of its 2024 revenue from Chinese operations over $300 million, while sister company CK Asset Holdings retained dozens of properties on the mainland, creating what analysts described as meaningful potential exposure to Beijing’s retaliation.

## The Philanthropist: ‘My Third Son’

To understand Li Ka-shing purely as a business strategist or geopolitical chess player is to miss a significant dimension of his identity. He is, by almost any measure, one of the most consequential philanthropists in Asian history.

He established the Li Ka Shing Foundation in 1980, focusing initially on education and medical research. In 1981, he founded Shantou University in his ancestral Guangdong province, the only privately-funded public university in mainland China. He has referred to the Foundation, to which he has allocated one-third of his total assets, as ‘my third son.’ As of the mid-2020s, his cumulative charitable giving has exceeded $3.8 billion, with more than 80% directed toward Greater China.

His educational philanthropy spans continents. Li Ka Shing-endowed professorships and research facilities exist at the University of Oxford, the University of Calgary, and the University of Hong Kong, among others. Two researchers he supported Professor Michael Houghton and Professor Jennifer Doudna received Nobel Prizes in 2020.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, the Foundation donated over HK$200 million in Hong Kong alone for medical relief and social welfare support, including PPE for frontline workers and financial assistance to patients ineligible for government subsidies. Li has described his philanthropic mission not as charity in the conventional sense, but as an investment in human capacity — a view that reflects the same long-term calculus that has defined his business career.

## The Hesitances: What Li Has Never Said

Despite decades of living at the center of some of the most consequential events in modern Asian history, Li Ka-shing has remained personally opaque to a degree that continues to frustrate journalists, analysts, and biographers alike. He rarely gives extended interviews. He does not maintain a social media presence. He has never written a memoir. His public statements — on politics, on China, on his own legacy — tend toward the philosophical and deliberately inconclusive.

He has not publicly commented on the 2019 protests beyond his careful initial statement. He has not commented publicly on the Panama ports controversy beyond the controlled silence of his company’s formal communications. When CK Hutchison released its 2024 annual results in March 2025 — at the very height of the ports crisis — the company did not hold a press conference and issued no statement addressing the deal. Victor Li’s chairman’s statement acknowledged only that the operating environment was ‘volatile and unpredictable.’

He has declined to clarify his relationship with the Chinese Communist Party, either to distance himself from it or to affirm loyalty. He has deflected questions about the 1996 kidnapping of Victor and the ransom’s payment. He has not spoken publicly about the death of his wife. He has not confirmed the nature of the back-channel discussions his son reportedly had with Chinese national leaders in 2025.

Those who have observed Li across decades suggest that this silence is not evasion but strategy — that every word Li Ka-shing has never said is as deliberate as every deal he has ever made. In a world of loudly performative billionaires, Li’s opacity is itself a kind of power.

## Legacy: The Last of His Kind?

Li Ka-shing turned 96 in July 2024. He remains listed as senior advisor to CK Hutchison and CK Asset Holdings, but real operational authority now rests with Victor. His health is not publicly discussed. He has not been seen at major public events in recent years, and his appearances even at family-controlled corporate functions have grown increasingly rare.

Some analysts argue he represents the last of a generation of Hong Kong Chinese tycoons who were able to accumulate extraordinary wealth precisely because they operated in the ambiguous space between the British colonial system and the emerging power of the People’s Republic — bridging East and West in ways that no longer seem structurally possible in today’s sharply bifurcated world. Jack Ma, the closest Chinese equivalent, was effectively sidelined by Beijing in 2020 after a public speech criticizing Chinese regulators. Li Ka-shing has spent a decade quietly avoiding a similar fate, apparently with greater success.

Whether the Panama ports deal ultimately succeeds — subject to Beijing’s regulatory review, U.S. political conditions, and the deal’s own complex legal architecture — or collapses under combined political pressure from both superpowers, it has already achieved something: it has reminded the world that Li Ka-shing, at 96, still has the capacity to surprise.

He arrived in Hong Kong with nothing. He built, over eight decades, an empire that spans continents. And now, as two superpowers argue over what he may or may not owe them, Li Ka-shing appears to be doing what he has always done: quietly, methodically, deciding for himself.

### Key Facts at a Glance

Born: July 29, 1928, Chao’an, Guangdong, China

Net Worth (2024): Approximately $37 — 38 billion (Forbes)

Nickname: ‘Superman’

Companies: CK Hutchison Holdings, CK Asset Holdings (via Victor Li), PCCW (via Richard Li)

Sons: Victor Li Tzar-kuoi (b. 1964), Richard Li Tzar-kai (b. 1966)

Philanthropy: Over $3.8 billion donated globally; Li Ka Shing Foundation (est. 1980)

Current controversy: $22.8bn Panama ports sale to BlackRock-led consortium (2025)
